labeler_notes: The provided passages do not contain an announcement of Tesla's next Gigafactory within the three-month horizon, and the snippets shown are unrelated cash flow, capex, debt, or other disclosures. Because an announcement would not necessarily appear in the specific obligatory lines shown, the claim is not settled by these passages.

The provided passages show capital expenditures and some references to Shanghai land-use/capital-expenditure obligations, but none explicitly state that Tesla had three new factories in place by the 12–18 month horizon. There is no obligatory disclosure line in these excerpts that would conclusively prove non-occurrence either, so the claim is not settled by the supplied passages.

The H1 2021 horizon has elapsed, but none of the provided post-call passages report the number of service centers opened. The cited capex/property-and-equipment cash flow lines do not necessarily disclose service-center openings or counts, so they cannot verify or contradict the specific claim of opening 46 service centers.

labeler_notes: The post-call filings show substantial purchases of property and equipment during the period, including $6.482B in 2021 and $7.158B in 2022, which supports that Tesla began/continued facility and equipment investment by the FY2022 horizon. However, the passages do not specifically tie the spending to a 100 GWh battery-manufacturing goal, so confidence is not high.

The horizon through FY2024 has elapsed, but the provided passages only disclose Tesla's aggregate capital expenditures/purchases of property and equipment, not Dojo-specific spending. Because total capex over $1 billion does not establish that Dojo spending was well over $1 billion, and no obligatory Dojo-specific disclosure line is shown, the claim cannot be resolved from these passages.

The provided passages covering FY2024 show only aggregate capital expenditures / purchases of property and equipment, including full-year 2024 purchases of property and equipment of $11.339 billion. They do not identify the specific construction project or state that initial phases of construction began in 2024, so the claim cannot be resolved from these passages alone.

The Q3 2024 horizon has elapsed, but the provided post-call filings only show aggregate capital expenditures/purchases of property and equipment through September 30, 2024 and do not disclose whether four additional 4680 assembly lines were installed or began installation in Q3. There is no obligatory disclosure line in the provided passages that would necessarily show this specific operational action, so the claim cannot be resolved from these passages.

The FY2024 10-K and interim 10-Q passages provided cover capital expenditures and other financial statement items, but none disclose the number of active H100 GPUs/training compute by year-end 2024. A capex line is not an obligatory disclosure point for the specific H100 count, so the passages do not verify or contradict the claim.

The available post-call filings only run through Q1 2026, while the claim horizon is FY2026 ending December 31, 2026. The passages indicate the Houston Megafactory was under construction, but they do not yet cover enough of 2026 to determine whether it launched during the year.

The claim is to start construction during FY2026, ending 2026-12-31. The only candidate filing reports the quarter ended 2026-03-31, before the 2026-04-22 call, so it cannot resolve whether construction starts later in 2026. No passage confirms or contradicts the specific research chip fab construction start.
